controlName: Conduct Comprehensive Health Risk Assessment
controlStatement: Who: Qualified Industrial Hygiene SME | What: Conduct health risk assessments for all tasks involving identified health hazards, estimate and quantify risks, determine appropriate control measures using hierarchy of control | When: For all identified health hazards, reviewed every 3 years minimum for high-risk tasks | Where: In proHealth IH or equivalent system | Why: To ensure health risks are systematically identified and controlled to ALARP | How: Assess exposure scenarios, evaluate OELs, select controls from hierarchy, approve and implement actions | Evidence: Documented HRA, approved by responsible manager, documented control implementation
riskAddressed: Occupational illness and chronic health effects from hazardous substance exposure
testingApproach: Verify HRA completion for all hazards, confirm exposure ratings below OELs, audit action tracking, assess field implementations

controlName: Manage Acute Toxic Substances with Enhanced Controls
controlStatement: Who: Asset/Business leadership | What: Identify designated ATS areas, mark areas with warning signs, protect with suitable PPE and gas monitoring, establish entry/exit controls | When: Upon identification and continuously | Where: At all locations with acute toxic substance concentrations at IDLH levels | Why: To prevent fatal acute exposure to highly toxic substances | How: Use DEP criteria to identify ATS areas, map and mark locations, implement gas detection as HSSE critical, ensure continuous communication | Evidence: Designated ATS area map, warning signs, gas monitor maintenance records, training documentation
keyActivities: Verify gas monitors as HSSE critical
riskAddressed: Fatal or serious acute exposure causing incapacitation, unconsciousness, respiratory failure
testingApproach: Review ATS area identification and marking, inspect PPE availability, verify training records, test emergency communication systems

controlName: Establish Health Surveillance Program
controlStatement: Who: Asset/Business | What: Implement health surveillance for workers with potential health risk from identifiable diseases, conduct baseline and periodic assessments, communicate results to workers | When: At baseline and periodic intervals per hazard requirements | Where: In protected health information systems | Why: To detect early signs of occupational disease and prevent progression | How: Identify workers requiring surveillance, establish baseline data, conduct periodic examinations, use validated techniques, communicate results | Evidence: Baseline and periodic surveillance records, communication documentation, retention for minimum 50 years
riskAddressed: Delayed detection of occupational diseases, cumulative health effects from chronic exposures
testingApproach: Audit surveillance records for baseline and periodic completion, verify testing frequency, review communication records, assess record retention and confidentiality

controlName: Identify Confined Spaces and Establish Register
controlStatement: Who: Asset/Business | What: Conduct survey to identify all confined spaces, maintain register identifying location, hazards, isolation requirements, entry procedures | When: Initially and updated continuously as new spaces identified | Where: In Confined Spaces Register | Why: To ensure all confined spaces are known and properly controlled via Permit to Work system | How: Survey facility for spaces meeting definition criteria, record in register, identify characteristic hazards and procedures | Evidence: Confined Spaces Register, periodic updates, training records on register contents
riskAddressed: Fatal incidents from unaware entry into unrecognized confined spaces with atmospheric hazards
testingApproach: Verify register completeness via facility walkdown, confirm Permit to Work applies, review training records for personnel knowledge

controlName: Implement Positive Isolation of Confined Spaces
controlStatement: Who: Asset/Business | What: Positively isolate confined spaces using air gap or blind flanges, lock-out/tag-out, verify isolation with independent competent person | When: Prior to any entry containing or that previously contained hazardous substances | Where: At isolation points as close to space as possible | Why: To prevent sudden release of hazardous substances into confined space | How: Plan isolation scheme, identify isolation points, implement physical isolations or blind flanges, tag and LOTO, verify by independent person | Evidence: Isolation Completion Certificates documenting scheme, location of points, verification that effective and positively maintained
keyActivities: Verify isolation design via process review
riskAddressed: Sudden release of hazardous substances causing vapor explosions, toxic exposure, asphyxiation
testingApproach: Review Isolation Completion Certificates, verify physical isolations exist, conduct pressure decay testing, interview isolation team on scheme rationale

controlName: Test and Verify Breathable Atmosphere Before Entry
controlStatement: Who: Authorised Gas Tester | What: Test atmosphere confirming oxygen 19.5-23.5%, flammability <=10% LFL, toxicity below exposure limits, document on Confined Space Entry Permit | When: Before entry and at routine monitoring intervals | Where: From multiple locations within space at various heights | Why: To prevent asphyxiation, explosions, and toxic poisoning | How: Calibrate equipment, test multiple locations, document readings, establish routine monitoring plan based on risk | Evidence: Testing logs with equipment ID, date, time, location, readings, monitoring plan documentation
keyActivities: Perform initial test at multiple heights
riskAddressed: Fatal asphyxiation, explosions from flammable vapor accumulation, acute toxic poisoning
testingApproach: Verify gas tester training and competency, audit testing frequency, inspect monitoring equipment calibration, review testing logs for completeness

controlName: Maintain Continuous Oversight of Confined Space Occupancy
controlStatement: Who: Competent Confined Space Entry Attendant | What: Remain outside space during occupancy, maintain record of entrants, monitor space and external conditions, stop work and order evacuation if conditions unsafe | When: Continuously while entrants inside | Where: Positioned outside space with visibility and communication capability | Why: To provide immediate response to atmospheric changes and detect incapacitation | How: Maintain sign-in/out records, monitor via communication system, check ventilation and gas alarms, scan for hazardous conditions | Evidence: Entry records with names and times, communication logs, incident reports documenting responses to alerts
riskAddressed: Undetected atmospheric changes, incapacitation, delayed rescue, secondary entrant injuries
testingApproach: Observe attendant positioning and communication, verify sign-in/out records maintained, audit communication system functionality, review incident reports for response timeliness

controlName: Develop and Rehearse Emergency Response/Rescue Plan
controlStatement: Who: Permit Requestor | What: Develop site-specific rescue plan addressing potential emergencies, defining rescue strategy, personnel roles, equipment needs, communication methods | When: Prior to work commencement | Where: In documented rescue plan approved by Field Manager | Why: To ensure capable rescue response if entrant incapacitation occurs | How: Assess emergency scenarios, plan rescue team composition and training, select and stage equipment, define communication plan, conduct rehearsal | Evidence: Rescue plan document, approval records, equipment staging verification, drill execution and observation records
keyActivities: Assess physical space for rescue constraints
riskAddressed: Preventable fatalities during rescue, rescue team incapacitation, ineffective rescue from inadequate planning
testingApproach: Review rescue plan completeness and scenario coverage, verify team training and competency, observe rehearsal execution, confirm equipment is present and functional

controlName: Ensure Equipment Free of Flammable Materials Before Hot Work
controlStatement: Who: Asset/Business | What: Ensure complete hazard-freeing of all flammable material sources using decontamination and isolation prior to hot work commencement | When: Prior to any hot work (welding, cutting, grinding, brazing) | Where: On all equipment subject to hot work | Why: To prevent fatal explosions and fires from ignition of residual flammable vapors | How: Apply decontamination methods (flushing, draining, purging, steaming), implement physical isolation (blinding or air gap), verify isolation through drain testing and gas monitoring | Evidence: Hazard-freeing plan, decontamination procedures, isolation scheme, isolation completion certificates, drain test results, independent verification sign-off
keyActivities: Identify all equipment/vessels subject to hot work
Design isolation scheme identifying all ingress points
Conduct drain tests and monitor for pressure buildup
Perform continuous gas monitoring during hot work
riskAddressed: Fatal explosions and fires from ignition of residual flammable vapors or hydrocarbon accumulation
testingApproach: Review hazard-freeing and decontamination plans, verify isolation schemes, conduct pressure decay tests, audit gas monitoring logs during hot work

controlName: Test Atmosphere and Monitor for Flammable Materials During Hot Work
controlStatement: Who: Authorised Gas Tester for Hot Work | What: Perform initial and periodic atmospheric testing, ensure oxygen 19.5-23.5% and flammability <1% not detectable, document in hot work permit, stop work if exceedances detected | When: Before permit issue and periodically per risk | Where: In hot work area with multiple sampling locations | Why: To prevent explosions and fires from flammable vapor ignition | How: Operate measuring equipment correctly, test multiple locations, establish monitoring frequency proportional to risk, document all testing, ensure equipment calibration current | Evidence: Hot work permit with all test results, gas detection equipment calibration certificates, continuous monitoring logs during high-risk activities
keyActivities: Perform initial gas test from multiple locations
Measure oxygen, flammable, and toxic levels
Document all testing with equipment ID
Alert work team and stop work if exceedances
riskAddressed: Undetected flammable vapor accumulation leading to explosions, exposure to toxic gases
testingApproach: Review AGT training and competency records, audit equipment calibration, sample gas test documentation, verify stop-work protocols when exceedances detected

controlName: Establish Fire Watch and Monitor 30 Minutes After Hot Work
controlStatement: Who: Competent Fire Watch | What: Maintain full oversight of hot work operation and monitor for minimum 30 minutes after completion to detect smoldering fires | When: During all hot work with open flames or heat/spark production; continue 30 minutes post-completion | Where: At work location with full view of operation and surrounding area | Why: To detect and suppress fires before escalation, including those developing in unnoticed locations | How: Inspect for flammable materials before and after breaks, display hot work permit, confirm firefighting equipment present, monitor gas detectors, maintain contact with work team | Evidence: Fire watch assignment records, firefighting equipment inspection records, incident reports documenting any fires detected and suppressed
keyActivities: Position fire watch with full view
Inspect hot work area before start and after breaks
Maintain communication with hot work team
Visually scan for fire sources during operation
riskAddressed: Undetected fires developing after hot work, delayed fire detection allowing escalation, fires in concealed locations
testingApproach: Observe fire watch positioning and vigilance, verify firefighting equipment present and operational, audit hot work permits for 30-minute requirement, review incident reports for fire detection timeliness

controlName: Categorize Lifts and Develop Risk-Based Lift Plans
controlStatement: Who: Person-In-Charge (PIC) | What: Categorize lifts using IOGP framework (Category 1/2/3) based on complexity and risk assessment, develop appropriate lift plans with technical details per category | When: For all lifting operations prior to commencement | Where: In lift plan document with approval | Why: To ensure appropriate planning rigor matched to risk level | How: Assess load utilization, lift type, proximity hazards, apply categorization criteria, develop plan with required detail level, obtain appropriate approvals | Evidence: Lift categorization documentation, lift plan with load/rigging/sequence details, approvals from PIC and L&H SME per category
keyActivities: Assess lift complexity using IOGP matrix
riskAddressed: Dropped loads causing personnel injuries or fatalities, crane failures, load instability
testingApproach: Review lift categorizations for accuracy, audit lift plan content against minimum requirements, verify L&H SME review for Category 3, confirm competency of approving personnel

controlName: Eliminate or Strictly Control Blind Lifts with SME Oversight
controlStatement: Who: Asset/Business | What: Eliminate blind lifts where possible; if unavoidable, require SME-reviewed plan, qualified banksmen maintaining visibility, in-cab cameras, clear communication, and clash checks | When: For any lift where crane operator cannot directly see load at any point | Where: In lift plan with blind lift controls specified | Why: To prevent load strikes on personnel, structures, or power lines | How: Evaluate alternatives to blind lift during work planning, develop detailed lift plan addressing visibility and communication, position banksmen to eliminate visibility gaps, establish dedicated radio communication | Evidence: Blind lift risk assessment showing elimination efforts, L&H SME approval of plan, pre-lift briefing records, communication test results, trial dry run documentation
riskAddressed: Load strikes on personnel, structures, or power lines due to operator inability to see obstacles
testingApproach: Review blind lift risk assessments and elimination efforts, verify L&H SME approval, observe banksman positioning and communication, audit incident reports for visibility-related near-misses

controlName: Apply Enhanced Controls for Lifting of Personnel
Who: Multi-disciplinary team | What: App: Man-riding certificates for cranes, test lift documentation, basket inspection records, L&H SME approval, trial lift records, rescue team readiness verification
keyActivities: Have L&H SME review and approve
Perform full load test with test weights
riskAddressed: Dropped personnel baskets causing multiple fatalities, equipment malfunction during personnel exposure
testingApproach: Review man-riding certificates, verify test lift documentation, audit basket inspection records, confirm L&H SME approval, inspect communications system, review rescue team readiness

controlName: Control Excavations Through Permit to Work with Risk Assessment
Who: Permit Issuer with Authorised Perso: Excavation Permit with risk categorization, attached risk assessment/JHA, Authorised Person certification verification, line locating certificates
keyActivities: Conduct site inspection for buried infrastructure
riskAddressed: Cave-in fatalities, strikes on buried utilities causing fires or explosions, atmospheric hazards
testingApproach: Review categorizations for accuracy, audit line locating records, verify Authorised Person competency, confirm risk assessments address all hazards

controlName: Prevent Trench Collapse Through Appropriate Slope/Bench/Shoring Design
Who: Discipline engineers | What: Design: Soil investigation and classification documentation, design calculations for slopes/benches/shoring, as-built verification by Authorised Person
keyActivities: Have Authorised Person inspect each stage
riskAddressed: Catastrophic trench collapse causing multiple fatalities, crush injuries, delayed rescue
testingApproach: Verify soil type determination, review design calculations, conduct stage inspections, compare as-built dimensions to design specifications

controlName: Assess Excavations for Confined Space Conditions Before Entry
Who: Authorised Person for Excavation |: Excavation Certificate with confined space assessment, signed AP confirmation, Confined Space Entry Permit if applicable
keyActivities: Obtain AP sign-off on safe-to-enter status
riskAddressed: Unrecognized confined space entry, oxygen deficiency, toxic gas accumulation, inadequate rescue capability
testingApproach: Review certificates and AP assessments, verify confined space determination, audit atmospheric monitoring if CS permit issued

controlName: Locate and Protect All Underground Utilities Before Excavation
Who: Asset owner and qualified line loca: Written approval from asset owner, line locating certifications, marked utility locations, positive identification (daylighting) photos and documentation
keyActivities: Contact asset owner and obtain approval
riskAddressed: Utility strikes causing fires, explosions, loss of services, electrocution, environmental damage
testingApproach: Verify line locator qualifications, review marked utility locations, audit positive identification records, confirm AP sign-off

controlName: Appoint Qualified Electrical Safety SME and Establish Rules
Who: Asset leadership | What: Appoint co: SME appointment documentation, verified competency records, published Electrical Safety Rules, training records on rule dissemination
riskAddressed: Electrocution and electrical injuries from inadequate safety oversight and unclear requirements
testingApproach: Verify SME appointment and competency, review Electrical Safety Rules documentation, confirm rule communication and understanding

controlName: Establish Electrically Safe Work Condition Before Work
Who: Competent and Authorised Person | W: Electrical Safe Work Permit, verification of isolation and grounding, test results with instrument identification
riskAddressed: Electrocution, cardiac arrest, severe burns from contact with energized conductors
testingApproach: Audit electrical safe work permits, verify isolation procedures, observe test equipment use and zero-voltage verification

controlName: Control Risks When Equipment Must Remain Energized
Who: Competent and Authorised Persons |: Electrical Safe Work Permit with working zone and PPE specified, incident procedure postings, PPE inventory matching required ratings
riskAddressed: Arc flash burns causing life-threatening injuries, electrocution from incidental contact
testingApproach: Review working zone determinations, verify arc flash calculations, audit PPE inventory and ratings, observe incident procedure training

controlName: Verify Driver Competency and Training
Who: Transport supervisor and training c: Valid license copies, training completion certificates, competency assessment records, driver medical clearance documentation
riskAddressed: Vehicle crashes and collisions due to untrained or incompetent drivers
testingApproach: Review driver license copies and validity dates, audit training completion records, interview drivers on knowledge

controlName: Assess and Monitor Driver Fitness to Work
Who: Occupational health and transport s: Medical clearance documentation, fatigue monitoring records, fitness decisions documented
riskAddressed: Vehicle crashes from driving while fatigued, impaired, or medically unfit
testingApproach: Review medical clearance documentation, audit fatigue monitoring and call-out records, verify fitness decisions documented

controlName: Ensure Vehicle Roadworthiness and Maintenance
Who: Fleet maintenance and driver respon: Maintenance schedule and completion records, pre-trip inspection checklists with defects documented, repair work orders
riskAddressed: Vehicle crashes and breakdowns from mechanical failures and poor maintenance
testingApproach: Review maintenance schedule and completion records, observe pre-trip inspections, audit defect reporting and repair completion

controlName: Develop and Execute Journey Management Plan
Who: Transport coordinator and driver |: Journey plan documentation, driver acknowledgment, weather and road condition briefings
keyActivities: Consider fatigue and driving hour limits
riskAddressed: Vehicle crashes from fatigue, poor route planning, unexpected hazards
testingApproach: Review journey plans for high-risk routes, verify driver briefing documentation, observe adherence to break schedules

controlName: Enforce Mandatory Seatbelt Usage
Who: Driver and passengers | What: Requi: Safety training records emphasizing seatbelts, vehicle safety rules documentation, incident reports showing seatbelt status
riskAddressed: Fatalities and severe injuries in vehicle crashes that would be prevented by seatbelts
testingApproach: Review driver training records, observe seatbelt compliance, audit incident reports for seatbelt use documentation

controlName: Appoint and Empower Dropped Objects Focal Point
Who: Asset leadership | What: Appoint co: Focal point appointment documentation, competency records, minutes of dropped objects management meetings
riskAddressed: High-consequence dropped object incidents causing fatalities and severe injuries
testingApproach: Verify focal point appointment and competency, review dropped object meeting minutes, audit register maintenance

controlName: Restrain Tools and Materials at Height
Who: Work supervisors and personnel | Wh: Work permits with restraint requirements specified, tethered tool inventory, incident reports with restraint status documented
keyActivities: Identify all items used at height
riskAddressed: Dropped objects causing fatalities and severe injuries to personnel below
testingApproach: Audit work permits for restraint requirements, observe restraint implementation, inspect tethered tool inventory and condition

controlName: Inspect and Maintain Overhead Equipment
Who: Equipment maintenance specialists |: Inspection schedules and completion records, inspection reports with findings, maintenance work orders
riskAddressed: Dropped objects from equipment failure or component loss
testingApproach: Review inspection schedules and completion records, audit inspection reports for completeness, verify corrective action closure

controlName: Establish Effective Exclusion Zones Below Work at Height
Who: Work planners and supervisors | Wha: Work permits with exclusion zones specified, exclusion zone markings and signs, spotter assignments if applicable
riskAddressed: Personnel exposure to dropped objects from work at height
testingApproach: Observe exclusion zone establishment and marking, verify signage and barriers, audit enforcement of no-entry policy

controlName: Conduct Dropped Objects Risk Assessment Pre-Work
Who: Work planners and safety engineers: Work permits with dropped objects risk assessment section, JHA with dropped objects hazards identified, control specifications
keyActivities: Identify all items that could drop
riskAddressed: Unidentified dropped object hazards resulting in injuries or fatalities
testingApproach: Review work permits and JHAs for dropped objects assessment, verify hazard identification completeness, audit control specifications

controlName: Appoint Competent Dangerous Goods Safety Advisor
Who: Company leadership | What: Appoint: DGSA appointment documentation, DGSA training and certification records, updated DGSA information to regulators
riskAddressed: Non-compliance with dangerous goods regulations, unsafe handling resulting in spills or incidents
testingApproach: Verify DGSA appointment and competency records, confirm regulatory notifications, audit DGSA involvement in compliance matters

controlName: Ensure DG Competency of Personnel Handling Dangerous Goods
Who: Training coordinators and superviso: DG training completion certificates, competency records, training renewal schedules and completion
riskAddressed: Unsafe handling and regulatory non-compliance from inadequately trained personnel
testingApproach: Review DG training records for all handlers, verify certifications are current, confirm refresher training completion

controlName: Classify Products and Prepare Compliant Transport Documents
Who: Product specialists and DGSA | What: Shipping papers with proper hazard classifications, product labels and placards, regulatory compliance verification
riskAddressed: Non-compliance with regulations, improper emergency response due to inadequate hazard information
testingApproach: Review transport documents for accuracy and completeness, verify hazard classifications, audit label and placard application

controlName: Assess and Manage DG Contractor Capability
Who: Contract manager and DGSA | What: A: Contractor assessment documentation, training verification, insurance certificates, safety management system review
riskAddressed: Unsafe transport and regulatory violations from inadequately qualified contractors
testingApproach: Review contractor assessments and qualifications, verify insurance certificates, audit contractor DG handling incidents

controlName: Report DG Incidents and Non-Compliance to Authorities
Who: DGSA and incident coordinators | Wh: Incident reports submitted to authorities, copies of filed reports, confirmation of receipt from authorities
riskAddressed: Regulatory non-compliance, loss of regulatory credibility, inability to respond to environmental hazards
testingApproach: Verify DG incident reporting procedures, audit filed incident reports, confirm regulatory authority submissions

controlName: Conduct Independent Building Safety Assessment Every 5 Years
Who: Independent competent engineer | Wh: Building assessment reports from independent engineer, remedial action documentation, follow-up inspections
riskAddressed: Building structural failure, collapse, and personnel injuries from undetected deterioration
testingApproach: Verify assessment schedule adherence, review assessment reports, audit remedial action implementation

controlName: Conduct Annual Fire Safety Risk Assessment
Who: Fire safety specialist or external: Fire safety assessment reports, equipment testing records, emergency procedure documentation
riskAddressed: Undetected fire hazards, inadequate emergency response, personnel casualties in fires
testingApproach: Review annual assessment reports, verify equipment testing completion, observe emergency procedure drills

controlName: Maintain Electrical Systems to EN-50110 Standards
Who: Electrical maintenance specialists: Electrical system maintenance records, inspection and testing reports, certification of compliance
riskAddressed: Electrical fires and electrocution hazards from inadequate maintenance
testingApproach: Review maintenance schedule and completion records, audit inspection and testing documentation, verify standards compliance

controlName: Develop and Maintain Building Emergency Response Plan
Who: Building management and safety coor: Emergency response plan documents, emergency contact posters, evacuation drill records and observations
riskAddressed: Unorganized emergency response, personnel injuries or fatalities from inadequate emergency procedures
testingApproach: Review emergency response plan documents, observe evacuation drills, verify emergency contact postings

controlName: Maintain Adequate Ventilation and Fresh Air Supply
controlStatement: Who: HVAC maintenance specialists | What: Maintain building ventilation systems to provide adequate fresh air supply and control temperature/humidity; conduct inspections and filter changes per schedule | When: Continuously per maintenance schedule | Where: In HVAC maintenance records | Why: To ensure adequate indoor air quality and prevent accumulation of hazardous vapors or contaminants | How: Establish ventilation maintenance schedule, conduct filter changes per intervals, inspect ductwork and vents, ensure fresh air intake operational | Evidence: HVAC maintenance records, filter change logs, air quality monitoring if applicable
riskAddressed: Poor indoor air quality, accumulation of harmful vapors or contaminants, health effects from inadequate ventilation
testingApproach: Review HVAC maintenance records, verify filter change completion, observe system operation

controlName: Stabilise, Slowdown, Shutdown Response
controlStatement: Who: Operations lead and console operator | What: Implement immediate SSS response (Stabilize, Slowdown, Shutdown) upon detection of abnormal situations | When: Upon detection or when situation deteriorates | Where: At control room and field locations | Why: To minimize process safety and reliability risks during abnormal situations | How: Follow escalation procedures and Master Alarm Database guidance | Evidence: Abnormal situation reports, shift logs, investigation documentation
riskAddressed: Uncontrolled loss of containment, equipment overpressure, process instability leading to safety incidents
testingApproach: Audit abnormal situation reports, interview operators, verify SSS implementation, observe drills

controlName: Master Alarm Database Consultation
controlStatement: Who: Console operator | What: Reference Master Alarm Database for alarm trip point, consequence of exceedance, and required operator response | When: Upon receipt of critical or standard priority alarm | Where: Control room console | Why: To enable rapid and correct identification of alarm significance and appropriate response | How: Access current MAD, review operator actions, validate alarm is real via secondary instrumentation | Evidence: Completed alarm responses, MAD query logs, shift reports documenting validation steps
riskAddressed: Delayed response to critical process deviations, incorrect operator actions leading to escalation of incidents
testingApproach: Audit sample responses, verify MAD accuracy quarterly, interview operators on procedure knowledge

controlName: Abnormal Situation Escalation Timing
controlStatement: Who: Production Team Lead, support staff | What: Escalate abnormal situations that persist beyond established thresholds | When: Critical Alarms uncleared >15 minutes; Standard Alarms uncleared >2 hours (unless MAD specifies otherwise) | Where: Via escalation process to shift supervisor and support teams | Why: To ensure timely intervention by personnel with additional expertise and authority | How: Initiate escalation per asset-specific escalation procedures, document reason and time of escalation | Evidence: Escalation logs, support staff notification records, resolution tracking
riskAddressed: Extended exposure to abnormal process conditions, delayed corrective action, loss of containment
testingApproach: Review escalation logs for timeliness, interview operations and support staff on escalation procedures

controlName: Fire and Gas Alarm Response Verification
controlStatement: Who: Operations team, field personnel | What: Upon Fire and Gas alarm notification, verify all trip actions have worked correctly per Cause and Effect matrix | When: Immediately upon Fire and Gas alarm receipt | Where: At affected areas and control room | Why: To ensure automatic safety functions operated as designed and personnel are protected | How: Check Fire and Gas Cause and Effect matrix, verify trip actions, confirm area personnel evacuation, assess need for additional actions | Evidence: F&G alarm investigation reports, personnel evacuation records, equipment trip verification logs
riskAddressed: Fire or explosion, personnel exposure to hazardous materials, failure of safety instrumented functions
testingApproach: Observe F&G alarm response drills, audit C&E matrix accuracy, interview operators on verification steps

controlName: Safety Instrumented Function Trip Verification
controlStatement: Who: Operations team, process engineer | What: Upon SIF trip, verify all final elements initiated by SIF have functioned correctly per Cause and Effect matrix | When: Immediately upon SIF trip notification | Where: At field equipment and control room | Why: To confirm safety function worked as designed and process is in safe state | How: Check Cause and Effect matrix, visually inspect trip actions, verify process parameters, assess need for stabilization or shutdown | Evidence: SIF trip investigation reports, field inspection records, equipment status documentation
riskAddressed: Uncontrolled hazardous condition due to failed safety function, personnel injury, environmental release
testingApproach: Audit SIF trip records, test final elements during maintenance, review investigation reports

controlName: Cause Determination for Abnormal Situations
controlStatement: Who: Operations team, support staff | What: Determine probable causes of abnormal situations through structured reasoning using information sources | When: During management of abnormal situation once immediate risk addressed | Where: In control room and via consultation with support staff | Why: To identify most effective corrective action and prevent recurrence | How: Gather information from MAD, P&IDs, operating procedures, registers, trends; use causal reasoning or 5-Why methodology; escalate if cause unclear | Evidence: Cause determination documentation, End of Shift Reports, After-Action Review records
riskAddressed: Repeated abnormal situations, ineffective corrective actions, misidentified root causes
testingApproach: Review cause documentation quality, audit effectiveness of identified actions, interview operators on methodology

controlName: Abnormal Situation Documentation and Reporting
controlStatement: Who: Console operator, shift supervisor | What: Document and report all abnormal situations with sufficient detail including description, cause, duration, actions, mitigations, and outcome | When: Upon resolution of abnormal situation | Where: In End of Shift Report and incident management system (Sphera, Energy Components) | Why: To ensure situational awareness, track patterns, support improvement, meet regulatory requirements | How: Complete abnormal situation record with required fields, escalate through proper channels, include work request for permanent repairs | Evidence: End of Shift Reports, incident records, work request documentation, comparison to MAD operator actions
riskAddressed: Loss of operational knowledge, unidentified patterns, regulatory non-compliance, repeated incidents
testingApproach: Audit quality of abnormal situation documentation, verify reporting completeness, track pattern analysis

controlName: Statement of Fitness Determination
controlStatement: Who: Production Team Leader, Production Support | What: Determine if Statement of Fitness is required following uncontrolled shutdown, overhaul, turnaround, or operation outside equipment constraints | When: When abnormal situation involves major incident or extended shutdown | Where: In formal SoF assessment and documentation | Why: To verify facility is fit for safe continued or resumed operation | How: Conduct structured review of equipment condition, barriers, and operational capability; document fitness assessment | Evidence: Statement of Fitness documentation, equipment inspection records, barrier verification logs
riskAddressed: Operation of damaged or impaired equipment, loss of barrier integrity, major incidents
testingApproach: Review SoF documentation, verify adequacy of assessments, audit against AIPSM requirements

controlName: Production Target Establishment
controlStatement: Who: FPP practitioner with production team confirmation | What: Define and document production targets that represent critical-few objectives required to deliver production plan | When: During monthly production plan development and operationalization | Where: In Operating Instructions document | Why: To provide clear production objectives that drive safe, optimized operations within constraints | How: Develop from production plan, validate within operating limits via multi-disciplinary review, communicate to operators | Evidence: Operating Instructions, production target documentation, target management tool records
riskAddressed: Misalignment between production planning and operations, value leakage, operating outside safe limits
testingApproach: Review OI for completeness, audit target management tool, interview FPP practitioners and operators

controlName: Process Target Development and Optimization
controlStatement: Who: Multi-disciplinary team (production, process, control engineers) | What: Develop, validate, and optimize process targets for every variable that could independently leak value | When: During asset care plan and target setting exercises, reviewed annually | Where: In Target Management Tool or equivalent system | Why: To establish operating ranges that maximize margin while maintaining steady operation within constraints | How: Follow nine-step target setting process, engage all disciplines, validate against ESP limits, embed in control systems | Evidence: Target setting documentation, completed target spreadsheet, console proactive monitoring screens
riskAddressed: Unoptimized operation, equipment constraint penalties, poor product quality, excessive energy consumption
testingApproach: Audit target spreadsheets, verify console screens, interview operators on target understanding, challenge targets on RCM/margin basis

controlName: Daily Production Team Performance Review
controlStatement: Who: Production Team Leader, console operator, process engineer | What: Conduct daily meeting to review production performance against plan, identify issues, and agree actions | When: Each shift or defined cadence | Where: In scheduled production team meeting | Why: To maintain focus on production objectives, identify barriers early, and coordinate actions | How: Review actual vs planned production, process targets, operating challenges, escalations, develop action items with owners and due dates | Evidence: Meeting minutes, action tracker, performance data records
riskAddressed: Missed production targets, unaddressed operational issues, poor coordination between teams
testingApproach: Observe daily meetings, audit action closure rates, interview on meeting effectiveness

controlName: Alarm Philosophy Establishment
controlStatement: Who: Asset engineering team | What: Establish and document Asset-specific Alarm Philosophy defining alarm management requirements and choices | When: Upon asset startup or at planned review intervals | Where: In documented Asset Alarm Philosophy | Why: To provide functional definition of alarm management lifecycle and ensure consistency with industry standards (IEC 62682) | How: Document alarm philosophy covering limits, priorities, operator responses, advanced practices, and maintenance requirements | Evidence: Approved Alarm Philosophy document, reference to DEP 32.80.10.14, training records
riskAddressed: Inconsistent alarm configuration, inadequate alarm responsiveness, failure to detect critical process deviations
testingApproach: Review Alarm Philosophy document, audit against IEC 62682 standard, verify implementation in control system

controlName: Master Alarm Database Development
controlStatement: Who: Multi-disciplinary team (process, control, HSSE engineers) | What: Develop and maintain comprehensive Master Alarm Database covering all alarms and trips from BPCS, IPF, FGS, subsystems, and third-party packages | When: Initially during design/startup, maintained throughout asset life | Where: In centralized MAD system/repository | Why: To serve as single point of truth for alarm limits, rationale, and operator responses ensuring consistent and safe operation | How: Perform identification, rationalization, detailed design phases, document all required fields per DEP 32.80.10.14 | Evidence: Current MAD document, version control records, audit comparison reports
riskAddressed: Inadequate alarm coverage, missed critical process deviations, operator confusion, inconsistent responses
testingApproach: Audit MAD against P&IDs quarterly/annually, verify limits against design documents, interview operators on MAD usage

controlName: Daily Alarm Review Process
controlStatement: Who: Operations, process engineering, production support | What: Review critical alarms, standing alarms, alarm overrides, and design/integrity limit exceedances from past 24-72 hours | When: Daily as part of Production Team Performance Review or Console Performance Review meeting | Where: In designated meeting/review process | Why: To identify emerging alarm issues, validate mitigation plans, and prevent normalization of abnormal alarms | How: Use alarm data, review registers, formulate action items with assigned owners and due dates | Evidence: Meeting minutes, action tracker, alarm statistics reports
riskAddressed: Normalization of alarms, ineffective control of nuisance alarms, uncorrected alarm issues
testingApproach: Observe daily reviews, audit action closure rates, interview team on bad actor resolutions

controlName: Alarm Performance Review Team (ART)
controlStatement: Who: Alarm Review Team (operations, process engineering, instrumentation/control systems, optional: pressure equipment, rotating equipment specialists) | What: Conduct periodic review of alarm rates, bad actors, standing alarms, alarm overrides, and design/integrity exceedances | When: Frequency based on alarm rate (weekly if >6 alarms/hr/operator, monthly if <3 alarms/hr/operator) | Where: In scheduled ART meeting | Why: To maintain long-term alarm system health and identify systematic problems requiring engineering solutions | How: Analyze alarm statistics, determine root causes, develop mitigations, track actions to closure | Evidence: ART meeting minutes, action log, trend reports on alarm metrics
riskAddressed: Persistent alarm problems, missed improvement opportunities, loss of situational awareness
testingApproach: Review ART meeting minutes, verify action item closure, analyze alarm rate trends

controlName: Alarm Override Management and Register
controlStatement: Who: Alarm override approver (Operations manager or designee) | What: Establish and maintain Alarm Override Register documenting all overrides, mitigation plans, and approval | When: Prior to implementation of any override, reviewed at defined frequency | Where: In accessible override register | Why: To document and manage risk of lost alarms while maintaining effectiveness of safety functions | How: Require formal approval, document rationale and mitigation, implement time-limited overrides, review register regularly | Evidence: Alarm Override Register, mitigation plan documentation, review meeting records
riskAddressed: Loss of critical alarm protection, missed process deviations due to standing overrides
testingApproach: Review override register, audit mitigation plans, verify time limits are respected, interview on override process

controlName: Control System Audit Against Master Alarm Database
controlStatement: Who: Control systems engineering, operations | What: Audit alarms in control system against current MAD to ensure alignment | When: At least annually, more frequently if project changes occur or discrepancies identified | Where: In control system and MAD comparison | Why: To detect and correct discrepancies between intended (MAD) and actual (control system) alarm configuration | How: Perform automated or manual comparison of critical/standard alarm limits, priorities, and status; track discrepancies to resolution via MoC | Evidence: Audit comparison reports, discrepancy tracking log, MoC documentation
riskAddressed: Unintended alarm configuration changes, loss of alarm effectiveness, non-compliance with design intent
testingApproach: Observe audit execution, verify discrepancy resolution, interview control systems team on process

controlName: Cumulative Risk Identification
controlStatement: Who: Asset leadership, frontline teams | What: Identify risk sources contributing to cumulative risk including CM/PM deferrals on critical equipment, temporary repairs, SIF overrides, high alarm rates, passing valves, MoC decisions, assurance findings | When: Continuously during operations, systematically at defined cadence (daily, weekly, monthly) | Where: In cumulative risk visualization tool or dashboard | Why: To understand interaction of degraded/impaired/missing barriers to prevent cascading failures | How: Collect risk data from multiple sources (CMMS, registers, dashboards), aggregate by location/barrier, assess interactions | Evidence: Cumulative risk visualizations, risk data reports, meeting documentation
riskAddressed: Undetected combination of barrier failures, cascading failures, major accident precursors
testingApproach: Review risk visualizations, verify data quality and recency, test decision-making at identified risk thresholds

controlName: Cumulative Risk Assessment and Decision Making
controlStatement: Who: Operations leadership, support staff, asset management | What: Assess cumulative risk by reviewing barrier health scores, interim mitigations, and impact on shift plan/operations | When: During frontline activities (permit release, shift planning) and at management cadence meetings | Where: In operational meetings and strategic reviews | Why: To make informed risk management decisions considering interaction of multiple risk sources | How: Apply Assess-Ask-Action framework at frontline (vet mitigations, confirm safety, request assurance, SSS, reprioritize) and at management level (review trends, identify improvement actions, resource allocation) | Evidence: Meeting minutes, decision logs, action follow-up records
riskAddressed: Poor decisions due to incomplete risk picture, continued operation at unacceptable risk, missed intervention opportunities
testingApproach: Interview decision makers, audit decision logs, review outcomes of cumulative risk-informed decisions

controlName: Procedure Scope and Categorization
controlStatement: Who: Document author, subject matter expert | What: Identify procedure scope and classify as Policy/Process, Procedure, or Job Aid | When: During initial procedure development and periodic review | Where: In procedure management system | Why: To ensure appropriate level of development, approval, and control based on associated risk | How: Define intended outcome and scope, align with risk using Shell Performance Framework RAM, assign criticality rating | Evidence: Classified procedure list, criticality assessment documentation
riskAddressed: Mismisclassified procedures, inappropriate control levels, unclear document purpose
testingApproach: Review procedure classifications, verify risk assessments, audit criticality assignments

controlName: Procedure Development and Verification
controlStatement: Who: Procedure author, SME, verifiers | What: Develop clear, concise procedures following big rules; verify content with executor and SMEs | When: During procedure development and revision | Where: In procedure document and verification records | Why: To ensure procedures reflect actual safe work practice and are usable by intended personnel | How: Author with executor input, conduct structured verification (desk review, HAZOP, field walk), obtain sign-off from competent verifiers (2 for critical/rating 3, 1 for rating 2/1) | Evidence: Procedure document, verification documentation, verifier sign-off
riskAddressed: Procedures that do not reflect actual safe practice, unusable or unclear procedures, missed life-critical steps
testingApproach: Interview procedure executors, audit verification records, observe procedure execution

controlName: Procedure Approval and Sign-off
controlStatement: Who: Approver per criticality level (PIC/OIM for critical, Production Specialist for rating 3, Supervisor for rating 2/1) | What: Approve procedure for use and establish sign-off requirements during execution | When: Prior to operational use and for each execution | Where: In procedure document and execution records | Why: To ensure appropriate authority approves use and to verify adherence during critical work | How: Risk-based approval hierarchy, sign-off at each step for critical procedures, place-keeping for lower risk | Evidence: Approval signatures, execution sign-off records, place-keeping documentation
riskAddressed: Unapproved procedures, unauthorized procedure execution, steps omitted or repeated
testingApproach: Audit approval records, verify sign-offs during procedure execution, interview on deviation handling

controlName: Step-7 Pre-Work Safety Conversation
controlStatement: Who: Work party members (any can lead, preferably rotated) | What: Conduct structured conversation prior to high-risk work (RAM severity 4+) to confirm safety readiness | When: Just prior to starting work | Where: At point of risk | Why: To verify barriers in place, build psychological safety, harden understanding of failure modes and mitigations | How: Discuss what can go wrong, what prevents it, what do if it does happen, verify controls present, agree readiness | Evidence: Step-7 Conversation Card completion, work party confirmation, supervision records
riskAddressed: Inadequate hazard understanding, missing controls, human error during high-risk work
testingApproach: Observe Step-7 Conversations, interview work parties, audit quality of conversations, verify barriers discussed are actually in place

controlName: Start Work Check Execution
controlStatement: Who: SWC Verifier (competent, not performing critical task, preferably not Permit Holder) | What: Verify controls are in place for 14 high-risk activity types via standardized pictorial checklist | When: Immediately prior to work commencement | Where: At work location with equipment/materials present | Why: To provide independent verification that controls are installed and understood before high-risk work begins | How: Use Shell-branded or IOGP 459-1 SWC checklist, visually verify controls, confirm with workers, Go/No-Go decision | Evidence: Completed SWC checklist, verifier sign-off, work party acknowledgment
riskAddressed: Undetected missing or ineffective controls, unexpected hazards during work, life-changing injuries
testingApproach: Observe SWC execution, verify controls identified are actually present, interview verifiers and workers, audit Go/No-Go decisions

controlName: Supervisor Span of Control and Competence
controlStatement: Who: Supervisor/Team Lead | What: Maintain manageable span of control based on permitted/non-permitted activity volume and risk | When: Continuously during work execution | Where: At work sites and in supervision chain | Why: To ensure supervisors can provide effective direction, coaching, and hazard control oversight | How: Determine span of control based on activity complexity/risk, select competent supervisors with technical and non-technical skills, provide supervision training | Evidence: Span of control documentation, supervisor competency records, training completion
riskAddressed: Inadequate supervision, missed hazards, inconsistent control, poor engagement/communication
testingApproach: Interview supervisors on span of control, audit competency records, observe supervision quality, gather feedback from workers

controlName: Work Control Procedures Development and Use
controlStatement: Who: Work planners, supervisors, executors | What: Develop clear procedures (or SOPs for non-permitted work) specifying work steps, hazards, controls, responsibilities | When: For all potentially hazardous work, reviewed at defined frequency | Where: In accessible procedure repository | Why: To ensure consistent, safe execution of work and control of hazards | How: Follow procedure development standards (intent clear, life-critical steps marked, detailed enough for competency level), train personnel, verify adherence | Evidence: Procedure documents, training records, execution compliance records
riskAddressed: Inconsistent work execution, missed hazards, human error, non-compliance with standards
testingApproach: Audit procedure quality, observe work execution, verify training, interview executors on procedure adherence

controlName: Hazard Assessment for PPE Selection
controlStatement: Who: Work planner, safety engineer | What: Conduct thorough assessment of workplace hazards to determine appropriate PPE requirements | When: Prior to work planning and periodically when hazards change | Where: In work plan or risk assessment documentation | Why: To ensure selected PPE provides adequate protection for identified hazards | How: Identify all hazards (chemical, biological, physical, ergonomic), determine exposure risk level, select compatible PPE, verify fit | Evidence: Hazard assessment documentation, PPE selection matrix, training records
riskAddressed: Inadequate protection from hazards, improper PPE selection, injury due to unsuitable equipment
testingApproach: Review hazard assessments, verify PPE selections match hazards, audit PPE training records

controlName: Contract Scope Definition and HSSE Risk Assessment
controlStatement: Who: Contract manager, technical representative | What: Define detailed scope of work with all discrete activities and assess HSSE risk using RAM | When: Prior to contractor selection | Where: In Contract HSSE Risk Assessment document | Why: To establish baseline risk for contractor capability assessment and appropriate control level | How: Define scope of work comprehensively, identify all activities, assess risk of each using RAM considering workplace context, determine Contract HSSE Risk as highest activity risk | Evidence: Scope of Work document, HSSE Risk Assessment, RAM analysis
riskAddressed: Scope ambiguity, inadequate contractor capability assessment, misalignment of controls to risk
testingApproach: Review scope documents, audit RAM assessments, verify against HER/technical standards

controlName: Contractor HSSE Management System Assessment
controlStatement: Who: Contract manager with SME support for high-risk scopes | What: Assess contractor capability to manage HSSE risks via evaluation of their HSSE management system | When: Prior to award for Medium/High Contract HSSE Risk, optional for Low Risk | Where: In Contractor Capability Assessment documentation | Why: To verify contractor has necessary systems, resources, and competence to manage contracted risks safely | How: Use IOGP 423-01 scalable questionnaire (gather-verify-assess), conduct site visit if needed, engage SMEs for high-risk activities, document assessment and any gaps | Evidence: Completed questionnaire, assessment documentation, gap analysis, site visit records
riskAddressed: Selection of incapable contractors, inadequate HSSE management, incident escalation
testingApproach: Review assessment documentation, audit against IOGP 423-01, interview assessment team, verify gap closures

controlName: HSSE Responsibility Allocation by Contract Mode
controlStatement: Who: Contract manager, operations management | What: Determine Contract Mode (1, 2, 3, Multi-mode) based on control of workplace and work activity | When: During contractor selection process | Where: In Contract Mode documentation | Why: To clearly establish HSSE management responsibilities between company and contractor, ensuring best-placed party controls risks | How: Assess control of workplace (access, operations permitted) and work activity (proximity, capability, authority), classify into Mode, document in contract | Evidence: Contract Mode determination document, contract clauses, responsibility matrix
riskAddressed: Unclear responsibilities, gaps in HSSE management, contractor-company conflicts over control
testingApproach: Review mode determination, verify alignment with control factors, audit contract HSSE clauses

controlName: High-Risk Contractor HSSE Plan Development
controlStatement: Who: Contractor (Mode 2/3) or Company (Mode 1) | What: Develop Contract HSSE Plan documenting approach to HSSE risk management for High Contract HSSE Risk scopes | When: Prior to contractor mobilization | Where: In Contract HSSE Plan document | Why: To translate contract requirements into specific, measurable HSSE controls and mitigations for contracted scope | How: Document scope summary, activity risk register, risk controls/mitigations, competent personnel roles, subcontractor management, verification plan, HSSE metrics | Evidence: Contract HSSE Plan, approval records, gap closure plans from assessment
riskAddressed: Inadequate HSSE planning, gap between contract requirements and execution, incident escalation
testingApproach: Review plan completeness, verify gap closures, audit against contract requirements, interview contractor on plan understanding

controlName: Pre-Mobilization Joint Risk Assessment
controlStatement: Who: Company and Contractor leadership | What: Jointly conduct thorough HSSE risk assessment incorporating all contracted scope risks and agreed mitigations | When: During pre-mobilization phase | Where: In Joint Risk Assessment documentation | Why: To ensure contractor and company have common understanding of risks and agreed controls before work commencement | How: Conduct structured risk workshop, document findings, identify actions, close actions before mobilization | Evidence: Joint Risk Assessment report, action tracking, closure sign-off
riskAddressed: Misalignment between company and contractor on risks/controls, missed risk mitigations, incident escalation
testingApproach: Review JRA documentation, verify action closure, interview participants on risk understanding, observe mobilization readiness

controlName: Contractor HSSE Performance Monitoring
controlStatement: Who: Contract manager, HSSE coordinator, technical representative | What: Monitor contractor HSSE performance throughout contract execution | When: Throughout contract execution, documented at planned intervals | Where: In contractor performance reports and meeting records | Why: To verify contractor maintains agreed HSSE standards and to identify and address performance gaps | How: Conduct site inspections, review HSSE metrics and incident reports, hold performance meetings, document findings, assign corrective actions | Evidence: Performance inspection reports, HSSE metrics tracking, meeting minutes, corrective action records
riskAddressed: Degradation of contractor HSSE performance, undetected non-compliance, incident risk escalation
testingApproach: Observe contractor activities, audit performance reports, interview contractor personnel, verify corrective action effectiveness

controlName: Equipment Care Strategy Definition
controlStatement: Who: Discipline engineers with Engineering Manager oversight | What: Define Equipment Care Strategies (preventive maintenance, surveillance, assurance tasks) using risk/consequence-based methodologies | When: Annually or when equipment/process changes | Where: In Asset Care Plan documentation | Why: To establish systematic, optimized maintenance approach that manages equipment failure risks while controlling costs | How: Apply RCM/RBI/SIS methodologies, include regulatory requirements, define task frequency/timing/method, optimize across equipment/system/asset levels | Evidence: RCM/RBI/SIS analysis documents, Equipment Care Strategy specifications, optimization documentation
riskAddressed: Inadequate maintenance, catastrophic equipment failures, unexpected downtime, quality/safety impact
testingApproach: Review RCM/RBI analysis, audit maintenance history against predicted failure modes, verify optimization rationale

controlName: Preventive Maintenance Scope Optimization
controlStatement: Who: Reliability Engineer, Discipline Engineers | What: Optimize preventive maintenance task timing, frequency, and scope to minimize cost while managing risk | When: During ACP development and annual review | Where: In Asset Care Plan | Why: To balance maintenance effectiveness with cost, extend equipment life, improve reliability | How: Apply MEI or cost-benefit analysis, optimize task grouping, shift work to on-the-run where feasible, benchmark with similar assets | Evidence: ACP documentation, optimization analysis, MEI calculations, cost profiles
riskAddressed: Excessive maintenance costs, poor asset reliability, missed opportunities for cost reduction
testingApproach: Audit MEI calculations, compare actual vs planned maintenance costs, review turnaround scope against ACP

controlName: Asset Care Plan Governance and Review
controlStatement: Who: Asset Leadership Team, Discipline Engineers | What: Govern and periodically review Asset Care Plan to ensure it remains effective and current | When: Annually or when significant equipment/operational changes occur | Where: In Asset Care Plan and governance meeting records | Why: To maintain ACP alignment with current asset condition, failure history, and business objectives | How: Conduct annual review of maintenance performance vs plan, update for failed components, incorporate lessons learned, document changes | Evidence: ACP review reports, decision logs, updated documentation, change tracking
riskAddressed: Outdated maintenance strategies, ineffective risk management, missed improvement opportunities
testingApproach: Review ACP update records, verify appropriateness of changes, audit compliance with updated plan

controlName: Production Planning Roles and Responsibilities
controlStatement: Who: Site Process Owner (SPO) with multi-disciplinary team | What: Define and communicate clear roles/responsibilities for production plan development and governance | When: At planning cycle initiation | Where: In planning procedures and team charters | Why: To ensure accountability, timeliness, and quality of production plan deliverables | How: Appoint SPO, identify key roles (support engineer, economist, scheduler, operations), establish timelines and milestones, communicate expectations | Evidence: Planning procedure document, role definitions, communication records
riskAddressed: Unclear accountability, missed planning deadlines, poor plan quality, conflicts between functions
testingApproach: Interview key roles on responsibilities, verify timeline adherence, audit plan quality and timeliness

controlName: Production Planning Model Maintenance and Governance
controlStatement: Who: SPO, technical SMEs | What: Maintain production planning models (PIMS, APS, IPSM, etc.) per modeling standards with version control and technical reviews | When: Continuously with formal reviews annually/3-5 years per risk | Where: In model documentation and change logs | Why: To ensure model output is reliable and represents current asset capability, preventing poor decisions based on inaccurate data | How: Apply modeling guidelines, document changes, conduct formal technical reviews covering structure/data/calibration, update based on operational feedback | Evidence: Model change log, technical review reports, calibration checks, audit trails
riskAddressed: Inaccurate production forecasts, poor operational decisions, missed business opportunities, unmet commitments
testingApproach: Compare model output to actual performance, review model change logs, audit technical reviews, interview modelers on calibration

controlName: Production Plan Development and Challenge
controlStatement: Who: Multi-disciplinary team (SPO, economists, schedulers, operations, technical disciplines) | What: Develop production plan using agreed base case inputs, run sensitivities, challenge plan in formal review before approval | When: At least monthly for rolling 90+ day outlook | Where: In production plan document and challenge meeting | Why: To generate realistic, optimized plan that can be operationalized and executed by operations while maximizing cash flow | How: Run models with verified inputs, review output for sense (material balance, constraints, margins), develop base/high/low cases, challenge plan, approve and publish | Evidence: Production plan document, sensitivity analyses, challenge meeting notes, approval sign-off
riskAddressed: Unrealistic plans, unexecutable operations targets, missed commercial opportunities, poor asset reliability impact
testingApproach: Review plan sensitivities, audit challenge meeting decisions, compare planned vs actual production, interview on plan realism

controlName: Production Forecasting Accuracy and Feedback
controlStatement: Who: FPP practitioner, forecasting specialist | What: Develop and maintain accurate production forecasts based on historical data and current operating conditions | When: Continuously updated during planning cycle | Where: In production forecasting documentation | Why: To provide reliable baseline for production planning and identify forecast accuracy opportunities | How: Analyze historical production data, assess current equipment/operational status, incorporate constraint data, calculate forecasts, track actual vs forecast for continuous improvement | Evidence: Forecast documentation, historical vs actual analysis, accuracy metrics, improvement tracking
riskAddressed: Inaccurate forecasts, poor planning basis, missed opportunities for optimization
testingApproach: Compare forecasted vs actual production, audit forecast methodology, review accuracy improvement initiatives

controlName: Asset HSSE Leadership and Commitment
controlStatement: Who: Asset leadership team (OIM, Production Manager, Plant Manager) | What: Demonstrate visible and committed leadership to HSSE and process safety management | When: Continuously through actions, decisions, and communications | Where: In all asset operations and leadership activities | Why: To establish strong safety culture and ensure HSSE is integral to business operations | How: Make safety-first decisions, visibly support safety initiatives, allocate resources for HSSE, communicate expectations, model safe behaviors | Evidence: Leadership meeting minutes, investment records, communication archives, safety performance data
riskAddressed: Weak safety culture, inadequate safety priority, poor employee engagement in safety
testingApproach: Interview leadership on safety priorities, audit resource allocation decisions, observe leadership behaviors, gather employee feedback

controlName: HSSE Policy and Objectives Definition
controlStatement: Who: Asset leadership, HSSE team | What: Develop and communicate Asset HSSE Policy and annual objectives aligned with company standards | When: Upon asset startup and annually for objectives | Where: In HSSE Policy document and objective records | Why: To establish clear expectations and strategic direction for all HSSE activities | How: Develop policy reflecting company standards and asset context, establish measurable objectives, communicate throughout organization, link to operational plans | Evidence: HSSE Policy document, objective documentation, communication records, performance data
riskAddressed: Unclear expectations, misaligned HSSE focus, ineffective performance management
testingApproach: Review policy and objectives, verify alignment with company standards, audit communication effectiveness, assess objective achievement

controlName: Daily Safety Observations and Go-See
controlStatement: Who: All personnel including supervisors, process engineers, operations staff | What: Conduct daily observations of work and operations to verify controls are in place and being followed | When: Daily as part of routine operations | Where: At work sites and operational areas | Why: To maintain constant awareness of safety compliance and identify potential gaps before incidents occur | How: Walk-around inspections, observe activities, engage personnel, document observations, follow up on any gaps identified | Evidence: Observation records, photos/videos, follow-up logs, trend analysis
riskAddressed: Undetected control gaps, normalization of hazards, complacency in safety practices
testingApproach: Review observation records, verify follow-up actions, interview personnel on observation process, assess observation quality

controlName: Operational Risk Control and Decision Making
controlStatement: Who: Operations team, Production Team Leader | What: Make operational decisions considering HSSE and process safety risks | When: Continuously during operations, especially for high-risk decisions | Where: In operational decision forums and permits | Why: To integrate safety into all operational decisions and maintain acceptable risk levels | How: Apply risk assessment criteria, use decision-making frameworks, escalate high-risk decisions, document rationale | Evidence: Operational decision logs, risk assessments, permit documentation, escalation records
riskAddressed: Poor decisions due to inadequate risk consideration, unintended consequence escalation, unacceptable risk operations
testingApproach: Audit decision logs, interview decision makers, review decision quality and outcomes

controlName: Periodic HSSE System Audits
controlStatement: Who: Independent auditors, discipline specialists, support staff | What: Conduct planned audits of HSSE management system implementation and effectiveness | When: Annually at minimum for major elements, more frequently for critical areas | Where: In audit plans and audit reports | Why: To verify HSSE requirements are implemented, effective, and compliant with standards | How: Develop audit plans covering all critical areas, conduct fieldwork, interview personnel, document findings, track corrective actions | Evidence: Audit reports, corrective action plans, closure documentation, improvement tracking
riskAddressed: Undetected compliance gaps, ineffective system implementation, missed improvement opportunities
testingApproach: Review audit reports for quality, verify findings accuracy, audit corrective action effectiveness, track improvement trends

controlName: Incident Reporting and Investigation
controlStatement: Who: Personnel involved in or observing incident, supervisors, safety team | What: Report all incidents regardless of severity and conduct proportionate investigations | When: Immediately upon incident occurrence | Where: In incident management system and investigation reports | Why: To understand causes, identify system failures, prevent recurrence, meet regulatory requirements | How: Encourage reporting with no-blame approach, conduct structured investigations using 5-Why or similar methodology, identify root causes and corrective actions | Evidence: Incident reports, investigation documentation, corrective action tracking, closure records
riskAddressed: Missed learning from incidents, repeated incidents, regulatory non-compliance
testingApproach: Review investigation quality, verify root cause identification, audit corrective action effectiveness, assess reporting culture

controlName: HSSE Performance Indicator Tracking
controlStatement: Who: HSSE team, operations management | What: Track leading and lagging HSSE performance indicators to monitor system effectiveness | When: Continuously with reporting at defined intervals (daily, weekly, monthly) | Where: In HSSE dashboards and performance reports | Why: To assess effectiveness of HSSE controls and identify trends requiring action | How: Define KPIs aligned with objectives, collect data systematically, analyze trends, report to leadership, drive improvement actions | Evidence: Performance dashboards, data reports, trend analysis, improvement tracking
riskAddressed: Undetected performance degradation, missed improvement opportunities, regulatory non-compliance
testingApproach: Review KPI definitions and data quality, verify trend analysis accuracy, audit performance reporting, assess improvement effectiveness

controlName: Management of Change Process and Implementation
controlStatement: Who: Change initiator, MoC reviewer, asset leadership | What: Implement formal MoC process for all changes to equipment, procedures, personnel, or operations | When: Prior to implementation of any identified change | Where: In MoC documentation and tracking system | Why: To ensure changes do not introduce new hazards or degrade existing controls | How: Identify changes, assess impacts, develop mitigations, obtain approvals, implement with verification, close out documentation | Evidence: MoC submissions, impact assessments, approval records, implementation verification, closure documentation
riskAddressed: Uncontrolled changes, introduction of new hazards, degradation of controls, inadequate hazard management
testingApproach: Audit MoC quality, verify impact assessments, review approval appropriateness, verify implementation effectiveness

controlName: Employee Competence Assessment and Training
controlStatement: Who: Supervisors, training coordinators, HSSE team | What: Assess and verify competence of all personnel for their assigned roles and responsibilities | When: Upon hire, periodically per role requirements, when role changes | Where: In competence records and training documentation | Why: To ensure personnel have knowledge and skills to perform safely and effectively | How: Define competency requirements per role, assess against requirements, identify gaps, provide training, verify competence | Evidence: Competency matrices, training records, assessment documentation, verification records
riskAddressed: Unqualified personnel, unsafe work practices, inadequate task execution, incident escalation
testingApproach: Review competency matrices, verify training completion, assess competence verification, interview personnel on knowledge

controlName: Process Improvement and Learning Organization
controlStatement: Who: All personnel, management team | What: Foster continuous improvement mindset and systematically capture and implement improvements | When: Continuously throughout operations | Where: In improvement tracking systems and action plans | Why: To steadily enhance operational performance, safety, and reliability | How: Encourage suggestions from all levels, assess improvement ideas systematically, prioritize and implement improvements, track benefits realization | Evidence: Improvement suggestions, evaluation documentation, implementation records, benefit tracking, lessons learned library
riskAddressed: Missed improvement opportunities, static risk levels, low employee engagement, poor organizational learning
testingApproach: Review improvement suggestions and evaluation quality, audit implementation effectiveness, interview personnel on improvement culture

controlName: Psychological Safety and Reporting Culture
controlStatement: Who: All leadership and personnel | What: Create and maintain psychological safety environment where personnel feel safe reporting concerns without fear of retaliation | When: Continuously through behaviors, policies, and actions | Where: In all asset operations and interactions | Why: To ensure hazards, concerns, and incidents are reported for early intervention and learning | How: Model psychological safety behaviors, maintain no-blame investigation approach, recognize and reward reporting, act on reported concerns | Evidence: Incident reporting rates, survey data on safety culture, follow-up on reported concerns, recognition records
riskAddressed: Under-reporting of incidents, hidden hazards, low employee engagement in safety, preventable incidents
testingApproach: Conduct safety culture survey, analyze incident reporting trends, interview personnel on reporting comfort, audit response to reports

controlName: Spill Response Plan Development and Maintenance
controlStatement: Who: Asset leadership, environmental team, operations | What: Develop and maintain comprehensive Emergency Spill Response Plans covering all potential spill scenarios | When: Upon asset startup and reviewed/updated annually or when significant changes occur | Where: In documented ESRP documents | Why: To ensure rapid and effective response to spills minimizing environmental impact and regulatory violations | How: Identify potential spill sources and volumes, define response procedures, assign responsibilities, establish communication protocols, maintain supplies and equipment | Evidence: ESRP documentation, resource inventory, training records, drill execution records
riskAddressed: Uncontrolled spill escalation, environmental contamination, regulatory violations, community impact
testingApproach: Review ESRP documentation, verify resource availability, conduct response drills, interview personnel on understanding

controlName: Emergency Response Training and Drills
controlStatement: Who: Emergency response team members, operations personnel | What: Conduct regular training and drills to maintain readiness for spill response | When: Annually at minimum, more frequently if identified as high-risk capability gap | Where: In training documentation and drill reports | Why: To ensure personnel are competent and equipment is functional when emergency occurs | How: Provide classroom training on procedures, conduct practical drills with simulations, evaluate performance, identify improvement areas | Evidence: Training completion records, drill reports with participation and observations, improvement tracking
riskAddressed: Unpreparedness during actual spill, ineffective response, wasted response time, environmental damage
testingApproach: Review training records, observe drill execution, audit drill reports, interview participants on competence

controlName: Marine Vessel Transit and Bunkering Operations
controlStatement: Who: Vessel captain/master, terminal operator, mooring master | What: Safely conduct marine vessel transits and bunkering operations within established operational limits | When: For all marine movements and transfers | Where: At port terminals and maritime approaches | Why: To prevent spills, collisions, and other maritime incidents | How: Follow maritime procedures, maintain situational awareness, monitor weather/sea state, verify cargo holds/equipment integrity, coordinate with terminal | Evidence: Vessel logs, mooring records, transfer documentation, incident reports
riskAddressed: Grounding, collision, cargo spill, pollution, loss of life
testingApproach: Audit vessel documentation, observe operations, interview maritime personnel, review incident reports

controlName: Spill/Incident After-Action Review
controlStatement: Who: Response team participants, operations leadership, HSSE team | What: Conduct After-Action Review following any spill or emergency response event | When: Within defined timeframe after event (typically within 48 hours) | Where: In AAR documentation | Why: To capture lessons learned, identify system improvements, prevent recurrence | How: Conduct structured review of what happened, what went well, what could be improved, document findings, develop improvement actions | Evidence: AAR documentation, improvement action tracking, implementation verification
riskAddressed: Repeated incidents, missed learning opportunities, ineffective response processes
testingApproach: Review AAR quality, verify appropriateness of identified improvements, audit implementation of actions
